mnemonics.title: LINKED LIST BASICS
mnemonics.content: NODES CONNECT BY POINTERS: DATA + NEXT = NODE. HEAD POINTS FIRST, NULL ENDS SEQUENCE.

mnemonics.title: LINKED LIST OPERATIONS
mnemonics.content: INSERT: FIND POSITION, ADJUST POINTERS. DELETE: SAVE NODE, MOVE HEAD, FREE MEMORY. TRAVERSE: HEAD TO NULL, VISIT EACH.

mnemonics.title: COMPLEXITY REMINDER
mnemonics.content: BEGINNING O(1), END O(N) (OR O(1) WITH TAIL), MIDDLE O(N), SEARCH O(N). ARRAYS: ACCESS O(1), INSERT/DELETE O(N).

acronyms.usage: Principle used by stacks, both array-based and linked-list-based implementations.
Principle used by queues, implemented using linked lists with front and rear pointers.
Building block of a linked list containing data and pointer to next node.
Pointer that references the first node in a linked list.
Pointer value indicating end of linked list (last node's next pointer).

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box divided into two parts: LEFT part labeled 'DATA' (stores value), RIGHT part labeled 'NEXT' (arrow pointing to next node). Arrow from NEXT shows connection to next node.
Draw boxes in a row (not touching). Each box has data inside and arrow from right side pointing to next box. Last box has arrow pointing to NULL (empty circle). HEAD arrow points to first box.
Draw boxes with THREE sections: LEFT arrow (prev), MIDDLE (data), RIGHT arrow (next). Arrows point both directions between nodes. First node's LEFT arrow points to NULL.
Like singly linked but LAST node's arrow loops back and points to FIRST node. No NULL at the end.
Draw new node box. Show new node's NEXT arrow pointing to current HEAD. Then show HEAD arrow moving to point to new node. Two-step process.
Two doors: FRONT (left) for exit, REAR (right) for entry. Items enter from REAR, exit from FRONT. Like a line at ticket counter.

analogies.analogy: Think of array as houses on a single street (contiguous), each with unique address. Linked list is like a treasure hunt - each clue (node) tells you where to find the next item, scattered across different locations.
Think of a train - each carriage (node) carries passengers (data) and has a connector (pointer) linking to the next carriage. The last carriage has no connector (NULL).
A stack of plates in a cafeteria. New plates added on top (push), plates removed from top (pop). In linked implementation, top of stack is the head of the list.
A queue is like people waiting in line at a movie theater. First person in line gets served first (front dequeue), new people join at the back (rear enqueue).
Removing the first book from a shelf and sliding all remaining books to fill the gap instantly - that's O(1) in linked list. In array, you'd need to physically move all other books.
Arrays are like fixed-size parking lots - once full, you need a bigger lot. Linked lists are like expandable train - add new carriages as needed without rebuilding the entire train.
